content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Alexandra Barnhill 
Jarvis Fay Doporto & Gibson, LLP 
492 Ninth Street, Suite 310 
Oakland CA 94607 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-052 
Dear Ms. Barnhill: 
April 4, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Hercules Planning 
Commissioner Hector Rubio and Councilmembers Susan Tolley and Gerard Boulanger regarding 
their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please 
note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), 
and any advice provided assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Further, we are only 
providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general 
conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Whether Planning Commissioner Hector Rubio and Councilmembers Susan Tolley and 
Gerard Boulanger have a disqualifying conflict of interest pursuant to Section 87100 with respect to 
an upcoming governmental decision regarding the Block N development project where their 
residences are more than 1,000 feet from the Block N project, but are closer to Block C-1, another 
project in the Master Plan Area? 
CONCLUSION 
The officials do not have a conflict of interest with respect to the design review decisions 
regarding the Block N development project within the Master Plan area, even though the officials 
own their respective parcels within 500 feet of, or slightly greater than 500 feet from, a separate 
Block C-1 project within the Master Plan area. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-052 
Page No. 2 
Background: On June 4, 2008, the Hercules City Clerk posted notice of the citizens' 
intention to circulate a petition requiring the Hercules Waterfront Master Plan Initiative to be 
submitted to the City Council for consideration. Completed petitions containing 3,666 unverified 
signatures were delivered to the City Clerk and the County Elections Department for verification. 
On July 14, 2008, the Contra Costa County Elections Department certified that the petition 
qualified pursuant to Elections Code Sections 9115, 9211, and 9214. Accordingly, the City Council 
adopted the Initiative as an ordinance on July 22, 2008. 
Pursuant to the Waterfront District Master Plan, the bayside area is being developed as a 
transit-oriented neighborhood mixed-use project. The District is located within the former Hercules 
Powder Company property in the City of Hercules, west of 1-80. The entire property encompasses 
approximately 167 acres, and is generally bounded by San Pablo Bay and the Union Pacific 
Railroad to the north; Talley Way, existing residential development and State-owned land to the 
west; the Remediation Financial, Incorporated site, Hercules Wastewater Treatment Plant, existing 
residential development, and the Citation residential project to the south; and the North Shore 
business park to the east. The Master Plan is comprised of several planning sub-area developments. 
The three officials reside within a 44-acre densely populated sub-area known as the Waterfront 
Neighborhood. Development of the Waterfront Neighborhood was completed during phase one of 
implementation of the Master Plan. 
Development of the 10-acre The Bowl and 22-acre The Village sub-areas have not 
commenced yet as all prerequisite permits must be acquired prior to implementation of the next 
phase. A project sponsor and developer, Ledcor Group, owns 16 of the 18 individual parcels that 
comprise The Bowl, Bayfront Boulevard, and The Village sub-areas. Specifically, Ledcor owns the 
project at issue in The Village known as District Block N. The Block N project calls for a mixed­
use development of 159 apartments, 16 townhouses, a parking garage, and approximately 6,000 
square feet of retail space that is currently in conformance with the Bayfront form based code and 
locally adopted building codes. Ledcor also owns another project in The Bowl that is 
geographically close to the residences of Planning Commissioner Hector Rubio and 
Councilmembers Susan Tolley and Gerard Boulanger, known as District Block C-1. 
You stated that the Planning Commission strives to ensure conformity with the form based 
codes and design review guidelines, rather than reopen the approvals of the individual projects 
within the Master Plan. Any subsequent development approval of the projects may require a 
deliberation by the City Council. Moreover, development of each project requires a deliberation 
independent of the other projects. 2 Thus, you have indicated that the approval of one of the projects 
has no bearing on the approval of another project regardless of common developer or owner. 
The Planning Commission will soon consider a streamlined design review analysis of the 
properties included within Block N. The distances from the officials' residences to the Block N 
project are as follows: 
2 The scope of this letter is limited to a subsequent City Council deliberation regarding the design review of 
Block N. Further developmental approvals are not contemplated within this letter. 


File No. A-17-052 
Page No. 3 
• Commissioner Rubio's residence is approximately 1,396 feet from the Block N project; 
• Councilmember Tolley's residence is approximately 1,465 feet from the Block N project, 
and; 
• Councilmember Boulanger's residence is approximately 1,261 feet from the Block N 
project. 
You also provided distances to Block C-1. In my phone conversation with you on March 20, 
2017, you indicated that Commissioner Rubio and Councilmembers Tolley and Boulanger have 
residences within 500 feet of the Block C-1 project. 3 However, as you stated, the matter in question 
does not include Block C-1. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, 
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. 
Section 87103(b) indicates that an official has a financial interest in "[a)ny real property in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000." You have identified that 
Commissioner Rubio and Councilmembers Tolley and Boulanger's real property is geographically 
close to Block C-1, such that their financial interests in their respective residences may result in a 
conflict of interest. We now consider the financial effect of the decision at issue on the officials' 
respective real property interests. 
Foreseeability: 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18701.) Under the facts you have provided, the officials" property is not explicitly 
involved in the decision(s) involving the design review of the Block N project. Thus, as applied to 
the officials' respective real property, Regulation 18701 (b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is 
reasonably foreseeable."4 
Materiality: 
As relevant to the facts here, the financial effect of the decision regarding the design review 
of the Block N project on Commissioner Rubio and Councilmembers·Tolley and Boulanger's 
respective real property interests is material if the decisions: 
3 According to Google Earth, Councilmember Boulanger's residence is approximately 564 feet away from 
Block Cl. 
4 Regulation 1870l(b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors as examples that may be considered in 
the "reasonably foreseeable" determination. 


File No. A-17-052 
Page No. 4 
-
Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic 
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real 
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other 
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official 
has a financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2( a)( 10).) 
-
Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property. 
(Regulation I 8702.2( a)( 12).) 
Commissioner Rubio: 
The facts provided do not indicate that the design review decision regarding the Block N 
project will affect the market value of his residence. Design review decisions generally concern the 
aesthetics of the properties in question. As such, those types of governmental decisions have little 
bearing on attendant noise levels, air quality, or odors that could feasibly impact the market value of 
the officials' residence. 
The pre-approved development of the Block N project may result in an increase in traffic 
levels because a parking garage and retail stores will be constructed as part of the project. However, 
the specific determination of the project will not have an impact on traffic levels because the 
potential impact was already determined when the development project was originally approved. 
Furthermore, Commissioner Rubio will likely be insulated from the immediate impact of 
any increase in traffic levels once development is completed because he lives in a densely populated 
residential subdivision six rows of houses and the Refugio Creek Corridor between his residence 
and the Block N project. Thus, the facts presented indicate that the decision regarding the 
streamlined design review of the Block N project would not affect the market value of his real 
property. 
Councilmember Tolley: 
Similarly, the facts provided do not show that the decision regarding the Block N project 
will change the market value of her residence either. Councilmember Tolley lives in the same 
densely populated residential subdivision as Commissioner Rubio in the Waterfront Neighborhood. 
The Councilmember is approximately 1,465 feet away from the Block N project. As such, her 
residence experiences the same topographical buffering that Commissioner Rubio' s residence does. 
Specifically, she is separated from the Block N project by six rows of houses within the adjacent 
development and the Refugio Creek Corridor. Again, a significant factor militating against the 
decision at issue affecting the Councilmember's real property interest is that the Block N project 
has already been approved. The facts do not indicate that the decision at issue will materially affect 
the market value of Councilmember Tolley's residence, if at all. 


Councilmember Boulanger: 
File No. A-17-052 
Page No. 5 
The facts presented do not indicate that the decision regarding the Block N project's design 
review will affect the market value of his real property interest either. The Councilmember is 
approximately 1,261 feet away from the Block N project. He resides in the same residential 
subdivision as Commissioner Rubio and Councilmember Tolley, and thus, he will experience the 
same topographical buffering from the Block N project as the other officials. Therefore, the 
decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable effect on his real property interest for the reasons 
detailed above. 
Project C-1: As noted above, the officials' proximity to C-1 is not a factor in this analysis 
for the following reasons: 
• You have indicated that the district block projects in the separate sub-areas are 
independent of each other and are not inextricably interrelated. The Master Plan 
encompasses a 167-acre bayside area much gr€:ater than the unimproved sub-areas that 
are currently being contemplated for development. Even though one developer has 
predominant control over the potential projects in The Bowl, Bayfront Boulevard, and 
The Village, the above facts support your assertion that the projects are independent of 
each other. 
• You have advised that all parcels require specific approval, and the financial 
performance of an individual project does not affect the financial performance of any 
other project. Theses facts center the operative analysis around the distance between the 
Block N project. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPO:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
Commission Counsel, 
Legal Division

sections.question:
Whether Planning Commissioner Hector Rubio and Councilmembers Susan Tolley and 
Gerard Boulanger have a disqualifying conflict of interest pursuant to Section 87100 with respect to 
an upcoming governmental decision regarding the Block N development project where their 
residences are more than 1,000 feet from the Block N project, but are closer to Block C-1, another 
project in the Master Plan Area?
sections.conclusion:
The officials do not have a conflict of interest with respect to the design review decisions 
regarding the Block N development project within the Master Plan area, even though the officials 
own their respective parcels within 500 feet of, or slightly greater than 500 feet from, a separate 
Block C-1 project within the Master Plan area.

Gerard Boulanger have a disqualifying conflict of interest pursuant to Section 87100 with respect to 
an upcoming governmental decision regarding the Block N development project where their 
residences are more than 1,000 feet from the Block N project, but are closer to Block C-1, another 
project in the Master Plan Area?

CONCLUSION: The officials do not have a conflict of interest with respect to the design review decisions 
regarding the Block N development project within the Master Plan area, even though the officials 
own their respective parcels within 500 feet of, or slightly greater than 500 feet from, a separate 
Block C-1 project within the Master Plan area.
